text: Nicole: Before we begin today is think about your 90 day planning plan from last time. Are you on track for it to be completed by next Thursday or are you off track? Just drop into the chat. On track, off track, on track, off track. Rescately, your background has changed. Again, On track or off track? If you're on track, have you started thinking about what you might like to include in your next nine day plan? What's your next area of focus? Remembering that I'm going to give you a couple. We're going to have a systems area, we're going to have a financial goal and then you're going to get to choose some for yourself. Daniel's on track with the changes made throughout this period. Nice work, Daniel.

Nicole: Not surprised. Rescately ever changing at the moment. On track for some aspects. Correct. Keeping you focused is one of the hardest jobs I have, Rees Gately. But you did make your calls. You sound very proud. I don't have to out you in front of everybody.

Nicole: Get to pat you on the back instead. Oh, Reece, well done. Congratulations. Okay, next question. When was the last time you checked in with your life and wealth planning plan? From the beginning of the year? Dylan's like, I don't know. What are you talking about? It's okay, you'll get to do one next year. Life and wealth planning day plan. When was the last time you checked in with it? Drop it into the chat.

Nicole: This week. Last week. Haven't looked at it since I did it. Didn't make it to the session to do it in the first place. Which one are you? Reese Gately. Well, you'll have the opportunity to Daniel, nice work. Check in with that before next week like you all if you made one to look in at where it's at. Hello, Eloise.

Nicole: Before next Thursday when we do our life and wealth planning. Sorry, our TIB90. Because part of your TIB90 should be reflecting where you want to be when we get to Christmas time this. Yeah. How much time do you want to be able to have off? How much profit do you want in the bank? What else was there that you were planning for? Do you have some significant investments you want to make in between now and then? Some of our people are cracking at it like Miles and Jackie. Others like Reese and Aaron haven't looked at it since they did it. That happens. Life gets away and before you know it, it's Christmas again and we didn't get what we wanted.

Nicole: So make sure you check in before next week, please. All right, I've got a few questions. But not everybody's here, so I'm going to start with the people that are here. Reece, Caitly, I'm going to begin with yours. You don't really have a question, but let's talk about getting leads. Would you like to. Oh, you did unmute. Thank you.
Nicole: Hello.
Nicole: Hi.
Nicole: Mostly to check that my stuff's working.

Nicole: Yes, it is. I was wondering whether you would do that or not. Tell me about your call. So for those who don't know, Reece's work is very sporadic. I would say, well, 80% of your work, Reece, maybe 70% of your work is like shutdown, breakdown work. So it's a little hard to plan for. But we are trying to make something a little more consistent for him. And so that has involved having to cold call businesses that may require his services.

Nicole: I'm going to tell you, if you've never done it before, it is horrible. It is not a fun thing to do. It is actually quite hard. It's uncomfortable. Particularly when you're an amazing person like Reece who really, really, really doesn't want to do it.
Nicole: Really, really, really don't want to.

Nicole: Well, I'm pretty sure we've been talking about this the whole time we've been working together, and finally this week we backed you into a corner and you have absolutely no choice but to make some calls. Great.
Nicole: Yeah, pretty much.
Nicole: Tell us about your experience.

Nicole: I mean, it hasn't been anywhere near as dangerous and concerning as I thought it has been. Sometimes I hit automated phone systems and can't quite figure out how to talk to a human being. There's been a few of those, but the. The few calls I made where I did actually end up talking to a person were reasonably positive whether they actually turn out to a job or not. As a whole different kettle of fish, but for the most part, it's finding the right person, having a bit of a jolly conversation with them, and that's about it.
Nicole: Race question for you. Did any of these people know who you were before you called them?
Nicole: No.
Nicole: That's interesting, isn't it?
Nicole: Interesting is one word for it, Nick. I was a little bit more annoyed than that, but yes, interesting is a word.
Nicole: So what have you been doing for your marketing where they may have been able to understand who you are prior to this?
Nicole: What's marketing?
Nicole: Thank you. So just for everyone on the call, Reese is in refractory. Do we call it refractory bricklaying or do we just call it refractories? How do I term this?
Nicole: Yeah, refractories.
Nicole: Okay.
Nicole: Either Is correct. But we're in refractories. But at least we're going to put something that people know about a very

Nicole: boutique service that anybody that requires it should know who Reese is because there aren't that many players in the market. There are some very big players, but Reese, correct me if I'm wrong, Violia, who's your biggest competition or the largest of the competition out there? They actually do things like rubbish collection. Like they do a whole bunch of different things, don't they? This is not their only thing that they do.
Nicole: The first two things they did to kind of get into Australian market was waste management and transportation. Buses and stuff like that.
Nicole: Wow.
Nicole: There was a while where Sydney was basically run by Veolia Buses and that was about it. If you're on a bus, it was a viola bus. So they're a very aggressive jack of all trades business.
Nicole: Okay. Which many, many others that yours is super attractive because you are not a big corp that does a bazillion things and doesn't specialize in anything.
Nicole: Exactly.

Nicole: Okay. So given that we've never marketed before and these people that we're calling don't even know who we are, how do you feel about the rest of the calls that you need to make? Is it still as intimidating?

Nicole: It's yes. Because I'm kind of. I mean, some of the responses have been positive in as far as people being like outside of the bruised ego, the responses have been positive from, I didn't even know you guys existed.
Nicole: Yep.
Nicole: Which has been good because that obviously means the cold call was worth the time because it's me throwing my hat in a ring that I wasn't previously in.
Nicole: Yep.
Nicole: Which was a problem I identified because dad, basically, there was no form of marketing. It was all word of mouth. And he took his foot off the gas about 15 years ago and just posted.
Nicole: Yep.
Nicole: So it's good to at least be at the very least getting my name back out there so that when things happen, mine is a name that people have actually bloody heard of.
Nicole: Yes.

Nicole: The. The hardest part for me and honestly the thing I've really struggled with, which is why I don't think I particularly care too much about the whole rejection. If people want to say no, piss off, whatever, that's. That's their prerogative, not mine. It's mostly because you only get one shot at a first impression. I think that's the hardest part for me. And so I need to show up like I'm. The businesses I'm competing against are multinational Conglomerates, you know, with cross T's, dot size, la la la la la.
Nicole: Whole marketing teams doing stuff that I'm just doing by myself and making it up as I go along. But I need to a competitor, not a dude with a ute who's pretty good at doing stuff.
Nicole: Who says?
Nicole: Me.
Nicole: And are you the authority here or. And is there another authority that might have a differing opinion?

Nicole: Can you just mute your mic for the rest of the conversation, Nick? Then yes. Yeah. And, and I, I think that is the thing, but it's just I want to make sure that when I show up, I'm showing up as my best self, my business's best self. And I'm seen as a competitor, not some dude. If that makes sense.
Nicole: It does make sense. And what happens when we wait for perfection?
Nicole: Yeah, well, how long have you and I been talking about me doing what I'm doing?
Nicole: That happens. Research. Actually, I'm going to do it right now and tell you how long it's been.
Nicole: I'd really rather it if you didn't because it had probably upset me more than the cold calls.

Nicole: The point here is nobody knows who you are until you tell them who you are. And yes, that's not necessarily a comfortable thing for any of us to do. In fact, it's pretty yucky. We have to expose ourselves. And you're right, it's, it's, it's a bump to the ego, that's for sure. Particularly when your dad has spent so much time doing this years and years ago. But when you think about the transient nature of. Because most of the people you're servicing, most, not all, are run by big corps.

Nicole: And so we have pretty transient people in big corps because they don't feel satisfied and they don't want to stay. So there's a perception that a big corp will only want to work with a big corp. It's like if you're working with a project builder. There's often a perception that you have to be a super professional business to get your foot in the door. But the opposite is actually true. Businesses want the appearance of a nimble business that they can work with. If I'm going to work with Veolia, it's a nightmare of hoops that I need to step through for us to be able to get someone onto site. In your case, you're nimble.

Nicole: You can make it happen today if we need to, nearly all of the time. And that's actually super, super attractive. And you do have all of the professional stuff in place. You've still got your safety stuff, you've still got all your charge out rates. Very easy for them to understand so they can factor in what that costing is going to look like. You have enough access to a team so that you can do the repairs. So I think this perception of need and you're going through ISO certification, which you've never been asked for, by the way, so that's just a step in the right direction. It's not necessarily a necessity just yet.
Nicole: I think that you have done a very good job of talking yourself out of being the right person to service their needs.

Nicole: One of the moderately positive conversations I had, I say moderately because the company I spoke to, really my target is the maintenance manager, is my A grade target. But the company I ended up talking to didn't have a maintenance manager at that point in time. They had lost one and hadn't recruited the new one. So I got talked to some, I was talking to some guy who just got pushed into the role. But that was the big thing I was trying to get across to him was they're planning a large shutdown at the end of this year. He said, that's already underway and so on and so on. But I said, look, you know where the guys that you call us, you need us there in two hours, we'll probably be there 99 times out of 100. And he goes, mate, if this place shuts down at 2am on a Sunday, we need someone there.
Nicole: And I go, mate, there's a reason my phone's next to my bed and it's not because I enjoy cuddling it.

Nicole: Exactly right. So I think the next biggest thing that needs to happen for you, and this is likewise with anybody that's building out a plan to work with builders, for example, or other businesses, we have that first conversation. The next thing that needs to happen is a reminder. So they need to. Particularly because your business name doesn't say what you do in would be very hard for them to remember and then go and Google you. So we need to find a way to leave them with an impression so that they have a reminder of who you are. And in your case, your best avenue for that is lumpy mail, something sent to them because you know who the person is now that you need to speak to in the mail as a. Not an envelope, but a lumpy parcel.

Nicole: That means they have to open it because nobody will resist opening a parcel. Now if that parcel, some stupid novelty thing that they leave on their desk, hooray for you because you've just Bought you the very best piece of marketing advertising space you could find. So perhaps it's coming down to what could I do? That means that they want to leave it on their desk. Not a mouse mask.

Nicole: To that point though, the one thing that the guy that I ended up talking to that didn't have the maintenance manager when he was like oh, what's the name of your company? There was a five minute conversation about why the hell we're named Wardrobe refractories.
Nicole: Great idea.
Nicole: Yep. And which also as much as I hate the name, I was like that's actually quite a funny marketing point because then we get into a conversation about the longevity of the business.
Nicole: Yep.
Nicole: But one of the things when I pulled out my 90 day plan that I haven't touched at all was lumpy mail items.
Nicole: Yep.
Nicole: Was something I was going to work on and get printed and get developed.
Nicole: Chat with ChatGPT today to see what amazing ideas it can come up with for lumpy mail for you. Knowing that the facility managers what are the titles do they have?

Nicole: Reese Maintenance manager, prod manager are kind of the two that I try to talk to the most. Is occasionally like boiler techs and boiler managers. But it's more often than not the maintenance manager and the boiler manager. The guys I want to be talking to.
Nicole: Perfect. Put that into ChatGPT. How do I get in front of these people? It could come up with in terms of a gift is something that could actually sit on their desk.

Nicole: One that Jane came up with that I really want to hunt a bit more. And also because it's very us is little basketballs like kid sized basketballs with the company on it. Because I would imagine that a lot of maintenance and prod managers have probably got young kids. Would be my suspicion. Just because the age that you'll typically hit that. So at the very least whether it sits on the desk or whether their kids out the backyard playing with it, either way it's front of mind. And Jane being the avid basketballer is a bit fun.

Nicole: Okay. So I think that's a great idea. And can I give some added feedback off the back of that please. If the basketball's at home with the kid. What? How am I going to remember you at my desk when I need you?
Nicole: Good point.
Nicole: So I don't think the basketball is a bad thing. I just also want something that's going to sit on the desk an and yes.
Nicole: I mean we've got the coffee mugs wallet.
Nicole: Yes.

Nicole: But these are forever old. I think dad got these printed about 15 odd years ago. But I want to find something that's different. I feel like everyone and every, you know, man and daughter's got a printed coffee mug.
Nicole: I'd love you to think about what sits on a desk.
Nicole: Yep.

Nicole: What could you do that might float the boat a bit? That actually literally sits on a desk. One of the best campaigns I did ages ago was send out these to all our ladies. Now, I regret that I didn't take the opportunity to put some branding on it, but realistically, you know, maybe you could do something similar. My camera's backwards and I don't like it. Sorry. But maybe there's an opportunity to do something similar that, you know, with a title plaque or a business card holder or a stationary holder thing, a pen holder, then, you know, there you go. Huh. That's a great idea.

Nicole: Dylan Builder of mine branded his hydro flask and hand them out to all the trades. Everyone drinks water is a great point. So as a pencil and pen holder with a logo. Yep. All those kinds of things are a great idea.

Nicole: Yeah. I mean, that's. I know I've got a few of those floating around, like drink bottles with. With brandy on them. And if you get the right ones. And I'm very pedantic about my travel T systems, so as long as you get the right one. Went everywhere with me for about six months.

Nicole: And that's another great, quick, easy idea. If you can figure out what time he's actually going to be in there. You could get some, like, keep cups made up. You know how you all fight over our TIB ones that are now all gone. You get some of those and have one delivered with a coffee in it.
Nicole: Or deliver it with a coffee in it.
Nicole: Yep.
Nicole: Myself.

Nicole: You can do it yourself. Yeah, absolutely. Or. Once upon a time, when we did sales calls here at tib, we would have. What do they call it? Air tasker. We would air task somebody, drop a coffee off right at the minute that the call started. It was so successful, was ridiculous, but just got very hard to navigate. So maybe you could do something similar.

Nicole: Yep. Okay. Anyone working with project builders, also a great idea. You could potentially, if you were emailing quotes as a builder rather than presenting them first, I'd slap you on the hand for not presenting them. But then if you did have to email them, emailing them with a bag of chips would be a great idea. I can see little ones with bags of chips, but also with a coffee so that it landed at the same time would be just a lovely naff little idea. Going to See you in a couple of weeks. This is very exciting.
Nicole: All right, thank you, Reese. Appreciate you being here and subjecting yourself to more coaching.
Nicole: Thanks, mate.

Nicole: Pleasure. All right, Dylan is asking about amex. Does anybody here use an Amex or use their credit card reward points or do anything with them at all? I personally don't have any credit cards, Dylan. However, I get smacked around by my financial advisor all the time for not having them. My accountant says, no, keep not having them. My financial advisor says, you're being stupid, you're missing out on all the points and the rewards. Here's some of the things I can tell you, Dylan. They changed the rules with the ato.

Nicole: I think it's like, I believe it's in this financial year so that they've gotten super, super tight on what you can use your reward points that you gather at work for without having to pay fringe benefits tax. And nobody wants to pay fringe benefits tax, which I'm gonna have to do that. So anything that is deemed a benefit. So for example, it actually makes a lot of sense for me because I travel for work almost every month, I'm flying somewhere. So if I'm gathering reward points that I can use to purchase my flights, that's not a fringe benefit to me because I've got to fly anyway and it's a business flight. But if I use those points to fly personally overseas for a holiday, that would be considered a fringe benefit and therefore in theory I would need to pay fringe benefits tax, which none of us want to do. So they've gotten really tight on this. In this current financial year that we're sitting in, whether that continues or not, I don't know if there is an opportunity for you to utilise a credit card or there's now another system called, what's it called? It's like Pay Go or something similar that I see advertised everywhere on social media to pay things like your BAS bills or your tax bills.

Nicole: If you've got the money there to pay it off. So you're utilizing the, the interest rate period appropriately and then you can use the, the points or the rewards within your company, then I'm a really big advocate for it. The problem I see is people rely on them and they become a problem. The credit cards become a problem rather than the asset we all think we're going to get. So if you feel as though you've got the cash flow, you've got the capacity to keep paying it off within its interest free terms and you're able to then use the Points within the business, then I'm a huge advocate for it. I think it's a great idea to give it a crack. Did you know I'm just going to mute you. Did you know about the fringe benefits tax stuff?
Dylan: I did. I didn't know too much about it. I probably chose to ignore it.
Nicole: Yeah, me too.
Dylan: I got a. I got the MX last year when I thought I was going on a. Overseas on a. On our. After we got married.
Nicole: Yes.
Dylan: And I thought, oh, I might be good just to get some points. But I didn't end up going overseas. I went up north. So.
Nicole: Yeah. Okay.
Dylan: They're just sitting there and I just. I don't know if it's worth it or not. I don't know.

Nicole: So I don't know. It probably just depends. Like, this is some of the reasons we have a family retreat in the WHIT Sundays where lots of our people are coming to the WHIT Sundays for a family holiday. So they can use it's. We do it all through TIB so that it's a tax deductible holiday for you. In theory. We're doing a two hour workshop which will actually just be a lot of fun. It won't be hard work like what we do here.

Nicole: So they get a holiday. Likewise, when we go to Perth for conference, lots of people come over and use their rewards for those kinds of travel opportunities. Because in theory, it's a TIB event that you're coming for. So if, if you're keen to use it for travel, then yes, it could most definitely work. And maybe your accountant. Like accountants are very. They're like a rainbow. Accountants are very black and white and some have lots of shades of gray and so some of them will help you find ways in which you can use this.
Nicole: So we don't trigger the fringe benefits tax.
Dylan: Yeah. My trip to Vegas was technically a business trip.

Nicole: You can always find a way to make it happen that way. I went to Milton was good for that too. Yeah, brilliant. Then I would have a chat with them about how you can best make it work for you without triggering the ftb. My advice to all of you. I had a really interesting conversation with my father this morning, of all people, because my brother's about to do one of the stupidest things I've ever heard and he's going to go and get himself a really big ram because he wants to be able to tow his caravan. He's going to do it through his business. He's a plasterer.

Nicole: There is a technicality with rams that means that there actually can't be a tax deduction. They can't be. You can't depreciate the asset. There's a whole bunch of technicalities around it because of the towing capacity. Because we all make assumptions around the towing capacity and in Australia, the way they're imported, the towing capacity actually needs to be upgraded for them to be able to be compatible, blah, blah, blah. There's no point me going into all of it. But my point here is he hasn't had a conversation with his accountant, so he's about to make a really big mistake thinking that it's actually a positive for his business. The point of that part is I want you all to be talking to your accountants before you make major decisions about this stuff because they're going to point, if they're worth their weight and anything, they're going to point you in the right direction.

Nicole: So a couple months ago, we told our accountant, okay, we need to upgrade our fleet. What are we going to do? Obviously, for ego, for appearance, for ease on the weekends, there's lots of different vehicles we would love to have, but most of them aren't going to serve our business in our tax depreciation opportunities best. So he tells us to go out and buy a D max. Well, that's what we've got. We've got a fleet of D Maxes. So this is when business can become a smart asset for all of us, is when we start to think about, well, what can the business do to facilitate us rather than the other way around when we're looking for those things that we want. Now, I'm not saying the business shouldn't give you what you want. It just needs to be done in a way that you understand for all of us that that might mean ftb or it might mean we can't depreciate and claim the depreciation, or it might mean a dozen different things for our tax.

Nicole: So just have those conversations and all of us get informed first. In your case, Dylan, if you're able to travel and log it up to the business, somehow, I definitely think it's worth looking into.
Dylan: All right, cool.

Nicole: Very good. Nobody else uses an A max or their points. I'm presuming not because nobody's given me any comments. As I said, my financial advisor every. And I speak to him twice a year, every year to do all my reviews. He slaps me on the hand every time I say no. I don't have any credit cards actually. So does my broker.

Nicole: He's a bit the same. Whereas my accountant's like, no, no, no, no, no. Sometimes I think that the other two have a different position than the accountant. So it's worth the conversation. There you go. Jane Reese's Jane has a St. George card personally and the points are great if he's turned them. She pays no fees and gets added benefits.

Nicole: The asset as a business owner is we're paying huge tax bills so we might as well use a credit card to use the tax bill and then use the points to upgrade your seats on the airplane so you're flying business class or first class or get a lounge membership or access to it or I don't know if it's 100 ways you can use it. Thank you. Good question. Okay, I've got a question from Cheney and Jordan. They're not here, but I'm going to mark down that it is 27 minutes in. So the question is or the challenge is running. Some advertising inquiries have increased and most seem to be A or B type projects, which is great because it's been a big stumbling block for them. But there is a sticking point in our sales process or perhaps their marketing where inquiries seem to be after quick quotes or same day site visits.

Nicole: When we send our inquiry form and checklist, they drop off. They don't want to make the effort to fill out the form. We also touch base with phone calls to chat through the form, but they have even found somebody else already or they ghost us all within one to two days. Thus far, our conversations, these new inquiries conversion is very low. What could we change in the sales process to ease the sticking point? The form seems to be too much of an effort or people are too busy and consumers want to want a site visit and they want that information before committing to a site. I would say if they're not prepared to fill out the form, they are not an A or B client. You guys are builders and none of your projects are going to happen that day or the next day. They're all going to take some time and that means to quote appropriately, it's going to take some time.

Nicole: So I would suggest that you're still not attracting A and B type clients. I would suggest perhaps the job types that they're talking about feel like they're an A and B job type. So in terms of what you could practically do to try and address this situation, I think it's more marketing around what your process looks like. Like, so where do the inquiries come from in the first place? Would be a good understanding to have if they're coming from social media, then use your social media to talk about your process and why we don't do same day quotes. This is why we have you fill out a screening form, call it something else, a pre visit form to educate them about the process so they don't make an assumption that you can do it on the same day. Anybody that's assuming that a builder or anybody that does any kind of project work full stop could do it in the same day is there's something wrong with them. That's not a you thing, that's them thing. The only thing you can control, guys, is being able to give them more of the story around your process and how you work before they even reach out to contact you.

Nicole: So that would be my suggestion for those people that are still coming through at the moment. Depending on how they inquire to you, you could either send out as an email, this is our process email to take them through, make it a visual so it's really easy for them to understand. That could go out with your screening form so they understand what it looks like and you could give them some timeframes within that which may assist. But again, I'm feeling like they're not actually the A and B clients. It's just the idea of the A and B projects that they're sending through. But they even go ahead or not would be very interesting to try and find out. Great question. Thank you.

Nicole: Dylan, I feel like we didn't get to see you last. Oh, that's because I didn't even do a session last week. Oh my God, I forgot. Thank you everyone for being patient with me. Dylan, you had another question from last week around social media and getting footage for social media. I'm going to ask Lara to answer this question for me because Lara has gone from having zero social media presence to having some of the best social media that I've seen in a really long time. And all of it, or nearly all of it includes Shane, not necessarily that Shane has done it himself. So Lara, what's the secret? Have you.
Nicole: Are you able to talk first? You're in a cafe, you got earphones on.
Lara: Can you hear me?
Nicole: Yes.
Lara: I don't know what the secret
Nicole: Shane's

Lara: not very good with. They ask him to take a photo of you and you get you and then all the background around. So I guess one of our photos and what looks like just a pipe in the ground. I then upload that photo, describe it and say information I don't know about our business social media post that I can attach these photo to and then something great. It'll ask you when it's your humorous voice, professional voice, it needs to ask some initiatives around it. However, Those posts are great for people who go and look at our page to see what we do, but they don't build relationships. And the posts that really take off and the post that customers say, oh, I saw that on Facebook feature Shane just being shame. So I will just go on site one day and say tell me a story.

Lara: And I give him any warning. If I give too many accidents about it too much, I just say tell me a story. And he's storyteller so he'll come up with something and I'll film it two or three different ways and then just choose whichever one kind of works best. If it's a bit long winded, I get my teenager to edit it down to not very much and I've just made sure that I've kept posted consistently and yeah, I don't know what else can I tell you.
Nicole: How do you get the photos in the first place? Is it always you having to go to site to get the photos I take?
Lara: You can tell the photos I take and the photos save.
Nicole: I can tell the difference.

Lara: But the photos Shane or the boys take are not necessarily bad. They just need a bit more information what it is. Especially if it's stuff behind the walls that people don't know or like. It just looks like a bike in the ground and then we have to put context to that photo. Whereas if I've gone on site to take photos, I know in my head I've already got an idea of what most is going to be. So I take the photo to match it. Whereas it's the other way. They're taking the photo and then I have to create content from the photo.
Nicole: Okay, that makes sense. Thank you, Lara. You did very well.
Lara: Also, I got a cat Lister from a teenager in the last couple of days. So simple. Yeah. There's templates. A bit like Canva. There's all the templates and things. Just that.
Nicole: Yeah, I like that.
Lara: And it asks you to upload four photos in a video and then it creates something. Yeah, with the overlay, everything.
Nicole: Yep. Super easy.
Lara: And dead animal swings.

Nicole: Laura had a post go viral. Was it last week or the week before? Because there's a dead animal in the post. Yeah. Some of the comments are freaking hilarious. It is very funny post. Thank you, Lara. I think the key creating the content. Dylan, for me, maybe I'll just talk about my perspective.
